methods.description: Process for conducting an archaeological underwater survey, identifying and documenting wooden piles, and collecting wood samples for subsequent analysis at the site of Ploča Mičov Grad.

steps.description: In the summer of 2018, a team consisting of the Institute for Archaeological Sciences of the University of Bern, the Museum of Ohrid and the Center for Prehistoric Research in Skopje resumed the research at the site of Ploča Mičov Grad and conducted an archeological underwater survey (Naumov et al., 2018).
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct archaeological underwater survey at a specific site.

steps.description: During this pilot study in 2018, an area of 40 m 2 (Field 1a) in the center of the previously determined pile concentration was recorded...
steps.searchable_summary: Record a specific area within the survey site.

steps.description: In water depths of up to 5 m, the lakebed was first cleaned of vegetation, and a 10 to 20 cm thick sandy surface layer with stones was removed.
steps.searchable_summary: Clean the lakebed by removing vegetation and surface sediment layers.

steps.description: After the removal of the sandy surface layer, numerous wooden piles protruding from the ground started to become visible (Fig. 5).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify wooden piles exposed after cleaning.

steps.description: During the 2018 campaign, all piles were provided with an individual identification number and documented in situ by means of photogrammetry ).
steps.searchable_summary: Assign identification numbers and document identified piles in situ using photogrammetry.

steps.description: For the subsequent dendrochronological analysis, a sample was taken from each pile (a slice of the whole diameter and of about 10 cm thickness was cut off with a handsaw underwater).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect wood samples from each documented pile by cutting a slice underwater.
outputs: Wood samples (slices ~10cm thick) from each pile

steps.description: In the area of Field 1a in total 265 piles were sampled, resulting in a density of 6.6 piles/m 2 .
steps.searchable_summary: Record the total number of sampled piles and calculate the pile density.

methods.description: Laboratory and analytical procedures for processing wood samples, measuring tree rings, cross-dating, and identifying wood anatomy to determine chronology.

steps.description: On all samples, an on-site preliminary species selection was performed (oak, juniper, other conifers, deciduous trees), the approximate number of annual rings for prioritizing the dendrochronological analysis, the shape (full section, roundwood, half section, wedge section, or worked on all sides), the presence of a waney edge (i.e. the last growth ring) and particular characteristics (e.g. chopping marks or charring) were recorded.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform preliminary assessment of wood samples including species identification, ring count estimation, shape, waney edge presence, and characteristics recording.
outputs: Sample metadata (species, estimated rings, shape, waney edge, characteristics)

steps.description: Additionally, all wooden slices were documented with photos and then vacuum-sealed for storage.
steps.searchable_summary: Document wood samples with photos and vacuum-seal for storage.

steps.description: The tree-ring width (TRW) measuring was carried out on a measuring table under a binocular microscope with the software Dendroplus (Ruoff, unpublished) and PAST5 (Version 5.0.610, SCIEM).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure tree-ring width (TRW) using a microscope and specialized software (Dendroplus, PAST5).

steps.description: The dendrochronological analysis was performed also with Dendroplus, which identifies cross-dating positons by using the standard dendrochronological statistical parameters, the percentage of parallel variation or Gleichläufigkeit (GLK) (Eckstein and Bauch, 1969) and the t-value (Baillie and Pilcher, 1973), and complemented by visual verification.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform dendrochronological cross-dating using statistical parameters (GLK, t-value) and visual verification with software (Dendroplus).

steps.description: The building of mean curves (MK) without any preexisting reference, i.e. without the possibility to check combinations of measured samples against a reliable standard curve, requires a particularly high quality of synchronization.
steps.searchable_summary: Build mean curves (MK) from synchronized tree-ring sequences.

steps.description: Additionally, the microscopic wood-anatomical features of the oak and conifer samples were identified...
steps.searchable_summary: Identify microscopic wood-anatomical features of samples (oak, conifer).

steps.description: ...and compared to standard reference literature (Akkemik and Yaman, 2012;Schweingruber and Baas, 1990).
steps.searchable_summary: Compare identified wood-anatomical features to standard reference literature.

methods.method_name: Bayesian Chronological Modeling and Statistical Analysis
methods.description: Statistical analysis of dendrochronological and radiocarbon data using Bayesian models in OxCal software to refine chronological information and visualize results.

steps.description: The obtained dendrochronological and radiocarbon measurements underwent statistical analysis. The radiocarbon dates were evaluated within Bayesian chronological models.
steps.searchable_summary: Evaluate radiocarbon dates using Bayesian chronological models.
outputs: Bayesian model evaluation results for radiocarbon dates

steps.description: This approach has proven to deliver high-quality chronological output information extracted from groups of radiocarbon dates when calibrated and modeled with specialized software, in this case the program OxCal v.4.4.2 (Bronk Ramsey, 2009aRamsey, , 2009b.
steps.searchable_summary: Calibrate and model radiocarbon dates using OxCal software.
outputs: Calibrated and modeled radiocarbon dates (posterior density estimates)

steps.description: Radiocarbon samples from tree-ring sequences of which the exact calendar-year intervals are known were used for 'wiggle-matching', i.e. a type of Bayesian radiocarbon modeling enabling a more precise chronological anchoring of 'floating' sequences (Bayliss, 2007;Galimberti et al., 2004;Bronk Ramsey et al., 2001).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform 'wiggle-matching' using Bayesian radiocarbon modeling to anchor floating tree-ring sequences.
inputs: Radiocarbon dates from known tree-ring intervals

steps.description: We also correlated radiocarbon measurements from organic remains in sediment layers with stratigraphical information, in order to narrow down the time intervals of the calibrated ranges.
steps.searchable_summary: Correlate radiocarbon measurements from sediment layers with stratigraphical information.

steps.description: Therefore, a bi-phased Bayesian model including phase boundaries was established.
steps.searchable_summary: Establish a bi-phased Bayesian model including phase boundaries.

steps.description: To calculate the duration of both phases a Sequence-Phase model was applied in OxCal.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply a Sequence-Phase model in OxCal to calculate phase durations.

steps.description: Standard Boundaries facilitated the phases' separation from each other.
steps.searchable_summary: Use Standard Boundaries in OxCal to separate phases within the model.

steps.description: In order to visualize each phase within the model, Kernel Density plots (KDE plots) were introduced (Bronk Ramsey, 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: Generate Kernel Density plots (KDE plots) to visualize phases within the Bayesian model.
